name: Dgraph Native Graph Database API
what_it_does: Open-source distributed native graph database with GraphQL and DQL (Dgraph Query Language) APIs — enabling developers to build highly connected data applications with native graph storage, horizontal scaling, GraphQL schema-based API generation, and ACID transactions, with Dgraph Cloud for managed hosting.
use_cases: Building knowledge graphs via Dgraph GraphQL API for connected data applications
Querying graph relationships via Dgraph DQL for deep relationship traversal at scale
Implementing recommendation engines via Dgraph for real-time graph-based suggestions
Building social networks via Dgraph for friend-of-friend and community analytics
Generating GraphQL APIs via Dgraph schema for auto-generated CRUD from graph model
not_for: RDF/SPARQL knowledge graphs (use Stardog or Amazon Neptune for semantic web standards)
Simple key-value or document storage (use Redis or MongoDB for simpler data models)
Traditional relational data with complex joins (use PostgreSQL for relational)
best_when: A developer needs a horizontally scalable graph database with auto-generated GraphQL API from schema definition, native graph storage (not adjacent tables), and open-source flexibility.
avoid_when: You need Cypher query language (use Neo4j), RDF/SPARQL (use Stardog), or production-grade managed cloud without self-hosting complexity.

auth.notes: Access Control List (ACL): username/password for ACL-enabled clusters. API key for Dgraph Cloud. Dgraph Alpha HTTP: /query endpoint for DQL. GraphQL endpoint: /graphql for auto-generated GraphQL. gRPC: port 9080 (Alpha) for Dgraph native protocol. Authorization: X-Auth-Token for Dgraph Cloud. Self-hosted (no ACL): open by default — must enable ACL. Slash GraphQL (legacy Dgraph Cloud): API key. Dgraph Cloud uses JWT internally.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Community edition: free self-hosted; Dgraph Cloud Free tier: limited storage/ops
pricing.paid_tiers: Dgraph Cloud: Starter $9.99/month; Launch $29.99/month; Enterprise custom
pricing.notes: US company (San Francisco). Founded 2015 by Manish Rai Jain (ex-Google). Dgraph Labs. $20M+ funding (Bain Capital, Felicis Ventures). Apache 2.0 open-source. Dgraph Cloud: managed (formerly Slash GraphQL). DQL (Dgraph Query Language): powerful graph query language with facets, aggregations. GraphQL: auto-generated from schema — easier for developers. gRPC: native Dgraph API for performance. Raft consensus: distributed ACID transactions. Horizontal scaling: sharding by predicate. Used by: companies building knowledge graphs, recommendation systems. Hypermode acquisition: Dgraph acquired by Hypermode in 2024 — AI-powered graph focus.

performance.rate_limits: Dgraph Cloud: tier-based limits; self-hosted: no limits

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: GraphQL + DQL APIs (no REST). X-Auth-Token for Cloud. ACL for self-hosted. gRPC for native protocol. Hypermode acquisition 2024. Apache 2.0 open-source. Free tier available. DQL knowledge required for advanced queries.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: GraphQL reads idempotent. Mutations: use upsert (query+mutation block) for idempotent upsert operations. DQL queries: idempotent. ACID transactions: coordinated multi-mutation transactions.
known_agent_gotchas: NO REST API: Dgraph uses GraphQL and DQL (not REST) — use /graphql endpoint or /query for DQL
DQL vs GraphQL: GraphQL is auto-generated from schema; DQL is more powerful for complex graph queries
Self-hosted ACL: disabled by default — ENABLE ACL in production (otherwise open cluster)
X-Auth-Token: Dgraph Cloud API key format — different from self-hosted ACL username/password
Hypermode acquisition (2024): Dgraph product direction shifting toward AI/LLM grounding use cases
Upsert pattern: use query+mutation block for idempotent graph mutations — standard Dgraph pattern

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced. ACL: must be explicitly enabled on self-hosted clusters. Open-source: auditable codebase. Dgraph Cloud: SOC2 certified. GDPR compliant. Self-hosted ACL disabled by default: critical security gap for new deployments. gRPC port (9080) must be secured. Graph data (social connections, knowledge relationships) can be sensitive. Hypermode acquisition: verify security posture continuity. Apache 2.0 license enables security audit.
